theme: Pre-board / mock examination performance as predictor of licensure success
subtheme: Mock/pre-board continuous scores predicting pass/fail (logistic regression, correlation)
study_design: Retrospective secondary-data descriptive and predictive analysis
population: 205 mock board records from a review center paired with PRC licensure results (Davao City, Philippines)
variables: Mock board continuous score (predictor); RTLE pass/fail (outcome)
key_finding: Mock board scores correlated with licensure result (reported Pearson r = -0.415 interpreted as higher mock scores → higher pass probability). Logistic regression B = 0.195, Wald = 30.424, p < .001; Exp(B) = 1.215 (odds increase ~21.5% per one-point mock score).
relationship_to_current_study: Directly relevant evidence that institutional mock/pre-board scores can predict individual RTLE pass/fail in a Philippine sample; provides analytic approach (logistic regression) and effect-size benchmark for modeling.
limitations_or_cautions: Study appears to include only review-center examinees; model included mock score only (no multivariable adjustment reported); inclusion/exclusion of repeaters and cohort years not fully specified (To be confirmed).
source_location: Tables and Methods: Pages 3-5 (Methods; Table 1–4 referenced in supplied content)
notes: Negative correlation sign reported likely due to coding of pass/fail variable; authors' text interprets higher mock → higher pass.

theme: Pre-board / mock examination performance as predictor of licensure success
subtheme: Pre-board alignment with licensure content and timing
study_design: Retrospective, multisite descriptive-correlational with multiple operationalizations and path analysis
population: 25 U.S. radiography programs; N=1265 graduates (HESI Exit Exam between Aug 2018–Jul 2021); ARRT first-time outcomes provided (scaled scores for subset)
variables: HESI Radiography Exit Exam scores (first, last, highest, average), program policy indicators; Outcome ARRT first-time pass/fail and scaled scores
key_finding: HESI Exit Exam scores positively associated with ARRT first-time pass rates across multiple operationalizations; program policies (minimum score, required prep) indirectly associated with ARRT outcomes via improved HESI scores (mediation).
relationship_to_current_study: Provides radiography-specific evidence that standardized pre-exit exams can predict certification outcomes and that program policies may function via improving pre-board scores. Useful for designing operationalization and policy-variable measurement in RT study; jurisdictional differences require local validation.
limitations_or_cautions: U.S. ARRT context differs from Philippine RTLE; sample may be biased toward higher-performing, accredited programs; program-policy timing across cohorts uncertain.
source_location: Tables 1–7, Methods and Results pages referenced in paper summary
notes: Useful methodological templates for multiple operationalizations (first/last/average/highest) of pre-board scores and for mediation/path analysis.

theme: Pre-board / mock examination performance as predictor of licensure success
subtheme: Pre-board alignment with licensure content and timing
study_design: Descriptive-correlational retrospective analysis (dentistry)
population: 49 dental alumni (graduated 2014–2018) who passed Dentistry Licensure Examination on first attempt
variables: GWA (predictor), pre-board comprehensive exam, pre-board practical, Dentistry Licensure Examination score (outcome)
key_finding: GWA correlated positively with licensure exam (Pearson r = 0.531, p = 0.000). Pre-board comprehensive exam did not significantly correlate with licensure performance (Pearson r = -0.037, p = 0.798).
relationship_to_current_study: Demonstrates that pre-board comprehensive exams may not always predict licensure outcomes; emphasizes need to verify alignment and timing of pre-board to national exam when using pre-board scores as predictors in RT.
limitations_or_cautions: Small, single-site sample and inclusion only of first-time passers (selection bias); limited pre-board practical data.
source_location: Tables 8–10 (Pages 5–6) per supplied summary
notes: Illustrates mixed evidence and potential selection bias in pre-board predictive studies.

theme: Institutional periodic assessments as predictors
subtheme: Monthly/periodic internal exams predicting comprehensive/institutional outcomes
study_design: Exploratory descriptive-correlational with multiple regression and SEM (census of cohort)
population: 26 RT interns (batch 2020–2021) at single HEI, Iligan City, Philippines
variables: Monthly exam scores (6 subject areas), case-study scores, Grit-S (predictors); Revalidation exam components (oral, practical, written) and total (outcome)
key_finding: Monthly exam scores strongly correlated with revalidation scores (r = 0.904, p < 0.001). In regression monthly exam score was significant (unstandardized β = 1.010, p < 0.001) with R² = 0.818. Case-study and grit not significant predictors.
relationship_to_current_study: Supports inclusion of frequent institutional written exams or pre-board-type periodic assessments as strong predictors of institutional comprehensive exam performance; indicates a large effect in this sample though the outcome differs from national RTLE.
limitations_or_cautions: Small single-site sample (n=26); outcome is institutional revalidation exam not the national RTLE; SEM stability limited by sample size; generalizability limited.
source_location: Correlation and regression tables: Tables 5–6, SEM fit: Tables 9–14 per supplied summary
notes: Useful for argument that aligned periodic assessments can explain large proportions of variance in institutional high-stakes tests, but mapping to RTLE must be empirically verified.

theme: Pre-board / mock examination performance as predictor of licensure success
subtheme: Large multi-program discriminant analyses including mock board as predictor
study_design: Quantitative predictive-correlational; discriminant function analysis with cross-validation
population: 933 examinees across five programs at University of Mindanao (2020–2025) — multiple degree programs (not RT-only)
variables: Nine predictors including fluid intelligence, communication, reading, math, professional knowledge, internship performance, mock board performance, study duration, academic difficulty; Outcome: PRC pass/fail
key_finding: DFA significant (Wilks’ Λ = 0.430; canonical r = 0.755; r² = 0.57). Classification accuracy original 88.4% (cross-validated 88.0%). Mock board and professional knowledge were among the strongest discriminators (unstandardized coefficients .053 and .074 respectively).
relationship_to_current_study: Illustrates a large-sample Philippines-based example where mock/pre-board and professional-course averages strongly discriminated passers from non-passers; methodological model (DFA with cross-validation) applicable to RT study though variable operationalization must be re-estimated for RT-specific data.
limitations_or_cautions: Multi-program sample includes non-RT disciplines; operational mapping to RT professional courses and PRC RTLE specifics requires confirmation; cross-validation method details To be confirmed.
source_location: Tables 2–8 and appendices per supplied summary (Pages 6–11 referenced)
notes: Provides operational definitions and supports including contextual covariates (study duration, repeats) in predictive models.

theme: Overall academic performance and professional-course grades as predictors
subtheme: GWA / professional-course averages predicting licensure
study_design: Descriptive-correlational retrospective (dentistry)
population: 49 dental alumni who passed Dentistry Licensure Examination on first attempt
variables: GWA, pre-board comprehensive/practical, licensure score
key_finding: GWA positively correlated with licensure exam score (Pearson r = 0.531, p = 0.000); GWA also correlated with pre-board comprehensive (r = 0.349, p = 0.014).
relationship_to_current_study: Supports including undergraduate GWA as a candidate predictor for RTLE; provides effect-size precedent from an allied-health Philippine context.
limitations_or_cautions: Sample limited to first-time passers and small single-site cohort; discipline differs from RT.
source_location: Tables 8–9 per supplied summary (Pages 5–6)
notes: Serves as supportive allied-health precedent for GWA predictive utility.

theme: Overall academic performance and professional-course grades as predictors
subtheme: Operationalization and measurement of GWA/professional knowledge
study_design: Large-sample discriminant analysis (see above)
population: 933 examinees across multiple programs
variables: Professional knowledge = arithmetic mean of final grades in professional education courses; GWA-like measures and cognitive tests also included
key_finding: Professional knowledge had the largest unstandardized coefficient in discriminant function and pronounced mean differences between passers and non-passers; demonstrates importance of defining professional-course aggregate.
relationship_to_current_study: Offers an operational template for computing professional-course averages from institutional records for inclusion in RT predictive models.
limitations_or_cautions: Mapping of specific professional-course lists to RT curriculum must be confirmed; grading scales may differ across institutions.
source_location: Research Instrument subsection; Tables 2 and 7 per supplied summary
notes: Useful for variable construction guidance.

theme: Clinical / internship / practicum performance
subtheme: Practicum/internship ratings as predictors
study_design: Discriminant function analysis in institutional dataset
population: 933 examinees (multi-program)
variables: Internship performance (final rating) included as predictor
key_finding: Internship performance had a positive but small unstandardized coefficient (.044) and small mean differences between passers and non-passers—suggesting limited discriminatory power relative to professional-course averages and mock board scores.
relationship_to_current_study: Indicates inclusion of clinical performance is appropriate but researchers should check for ceiling effects and restricted range in RT practicum ratings when evaluating predictive value.
limitations_or_cautions: High mean internship ratings for both groups may reduce discriminatory power; RT data may differ.
source_location: Table 2 and Table 7 per supplied summary (Pages 6–10)
notes: Warns about restricted range issues.

theme: Non-cognitive, attitudinal, and readiness measures
subtheme: Grit and other non-cognitive traits
study_design: Census sample correlational and regression analyses
population: 26 RT interns (Iligan City)
variables: Grit-S (Grit) as predictor; revalidation exam scores outcome
key_finding: Grit had no significant correlation with revalidation exam scores (r = -0.043, p = 0.833) and was not a significant predictor in regression.
relationship_to_current_study: Suggests grit may not be a strong individual predictor of written comprehensive exam outcomes in small RT samples; may still be considered for broader multi-variable models but expect small effects.
limitations_or_cautions: Small sample size limits power to detect small effects.
source_location: Grit descriptive and regression tables: Pages 7–9 per supplied summary
notes: Example of a non-cognitive predictor with null finding in RT-specific sample.

theme: Non-cognitive, attitudinal, and readiness measures
subtheme: Self-reported preparedness and attitude toward licensure
study_design: Descriptive-correlational cross-sectional survey
population: 54 RT graduates, Emilio Aguinaldo College - Cavite (batch 2024)
variables: Attitude, preparedness, perceived challenges (self-report); no objective academic/pre-board/licensure outcomes reported
key_finding: Attitude correlated moderately with preparedness (r = .562, p = .000); preparedness inversely correlated with perceived challenges (r = -0.290, p = .033). No objective academic or licensure outcomes reported in the study.
relationship_to_current_study: Provides context on graduates' self-perceived readiness and challenges; useful for descriptive background and for identifying potential non-academic covariates but cannot be used as evidence for prediction of licensure outcomes.
limitations_or_cautions: Self-report measures only; absence of objective outcomes explicitly noted.
source_location: Tables 1–6 and Tables 7 (interventions) per supplied summary (Pages 3–8)
notes: Useful for programmatic intervention ideas but not predictive evidence.

theme: Assessment design, psychometrics, and measurement issues
subtheme: Item construction and cognitive-level classification (Bloom taxonomy)
study_design: Prospective exploratory simulation using 110 image-rich board-style MCQs (AI as examinee)
population: 110 image-rich MCQs assembled per ABR blueprints; ChatGPT-4 responses collected
variables: Item cognitive level, domain, modality, difficulty; correctness and confidence of AI responses
key_finding: Paper demonstrates methods for assembling board-style items aligned to a blueprint, classifying items by cognitive level, rating difficulty, applying correction-for-guessing, and assessing reproducibility (Cohen's Kappa) — methodological examples relevant to constructing pre-board instruments.
relationship_to_current_study: Provides practical assessment construction and psychometric-check methods to apply when developing or evaluating institutional pre-board exams intended for predictive use.
limitations_or_cautions: Study used AI responses, not human examinees; items were not psychometrically validated; but methods remain applicable.
source_location: Methods and Results sections; Tables 1–3 per supplied summary
notes: Recommend applying item-level analyses (difficulty, cognitive level) and reliability checks to pre-board instruments.

theme: Institutional and cohort/contextual predictors (aggregate-level effects)
subtheme: Cohort size and institutional passing rates affecting failure counts
study_design: Retrospective longitudinal aggregated institutional analysis using Poisson/negative binomial regression
population: Aggregated yearly data (2014–2024) from five Philippine RT schools
variables: Total examinees, institutional passing rate, number failed first-time takers, failed repeaters, year (predictors); number of failed examinees (count outcome)
key_finding: Cohort size (total examinees) was a significant positive predictor of number of failed examinees in some institutions; institutional passing rate was a significant negative predictor in one institution. Predictors varied across institutions with inconsistent significance.
relationship_to_current_study: Alerts to the influence of cohort-level contextual variables; suggests including institutional/career timeline covariates or considering multi-level modeling if pooling across cohorts/institutions.
limitations_or_cautions: Aggregated data cannot substitute for individual-level predictor analyses; heterogeneity across institutions and missing-year data (e.g., 2020 exam absence) complicate interpretation.
source_location: Tables 1–6 and methods description per supplied summary
notes: Useful for institutional planning and controlling for cohort/contextual covariates.

theme: Program policies, remediation, and review practices
subtheme: Minimum pre-board score requirements and required preparation
study_design: Multisite retrospective correlational with program policy survey and path analysis
population: 25 U.S. radiography programs; N=1265 graduates
variables: HESI scores; program policy indicators (minimum score required, required test prep, remediation); ARRT outcomes
key_finding: Programs with policies requiring minimum HESI scores and required preparation had higher HESI scores and higher ARRT pass rates; path analysis indicated policies influenced ARRT outcomes indirectly via HESI performance.
relationship_to_current_study: Supports measuring program-level policy variables and considering mediation by pre-board performance when interpreting institutional differences in RT licensure outcomes.
limitations_or_cautions: Context differs (U.S./ARRT) and participating programs tended to be higher-performing; temporal alignment of policies to cohorts uncertain.
source_location: Path analysis and policy tables referenced in paper summary
notes: Encourages capturing policy/practice variables (e.g., mandatory mock, remediation) as possible mediators/moderators in predictive models.

theme: Overall academic performance and professional-course grades as predictors
subtheme: Admission test vs licensure outcomes
study_design: Retrospective correlational institutional records (AY2015–2023)
population: 178 BSRT students, AY 2015–2023 (Registrar and Guidance office records)
variables: Admission test Stanine (predictor); Professional licensure examination average rating by year (outcome)
key_finding: Reported weak relationship between admission test stanine and average professional licensure rating for the cohort (exact correlation coefficient and p-value not reported in supplied extract).
relationship_to_current_study: Suggests admission-test indicators may have limited predictive value relative to in-program academic and pre-board measures; relevant when selecting predictor candidate sets.
limitations_or_cautions: Exact statistics and p-values are To be confirmed; missing years (2018, 2020) due to COVID-19 noted; details on whether outcome is first-taker average or other are To be confirmed.
source_location: Summary and methods notes per supplied evidence_table extract (Pages 1–5 referenced)
notes: Useful cautionary evidence about admission-test predictive value; recommend confirming raw statistics in source.

theme: Assessment design, psychometrics, and measurement issues
subtheme: Reproducibility and reliability of assessment instruments
study_design: Prospective exploratory simulation; two runs to assess reproducibility
population: 110 image-rich MCQs; two separate runs/accounts producing responses
variables: Answer correctness per run; reproducibility measures; Cohen's Kappa
key_finding: Answers differed on 14% of questions between runs; Cohen's Kappa = .73 indicating substantial agreement; highlights need to assess reproducibility and reliability of assessment instruments.
relationship_to_current_study: Informative for evaluating reliability of pre-board instruments (repeatability, inter-rater/run agreement) before using them as predictive inputs.
limitations_or_cautions: AI study context; human-administered exams may have different reliability characteristics, but conceptually applicable.
source_location: Reproducibility results and Cohen's Kappa: Pages 11–12 and Table 3 per supplied summary
notes: Recommend reporting reproducibility metrics for pre-board exams.

theme: Pre-board / mock examination performance as predictor of licensure success
subtheme: Programmatic implications and enhancement programs
study_design: Retrospective paired-analysis with program recommendations
population: 205 mock board records paired with PRC results (Davao City)
variables: Mock board scores and proposed enhancement interventions
key_finding: Authors proposed an enhancement program (weekly mock/comprehensive tests, peer mentoring, intensified review phases) based on the observed mock→licensure association; interventions not empirically tested within the study.
relationship_to_current_study: Provides programmatic interventions that could be assessed prospectively or included as covariates (review participation) when modeling licensure outcomes.
limitations_or_cautions: Interventions are prescriptive; no evaluation data provided in the source.
source_location: Recommendations and Enhancement Program sections (Pages 6–8) per supplied summary
notes: Useful for discussing practical uses of predictive models for remediation planning.

theme: Institutional and cohort/contextual predictors (aggregate-level effects)
subtheme: Yearly trends and missing data impacts
study_design: Retrospective longitudinal institutional analysis (2014–2024)
population: Five RT schools, yearly aggregated counts 2014–2024
variables: Institutional passing rates, total examinees, failed examinees (by first-taker vs repeater), year
key_finding: Institutional performance varied across years and institutions; national passing rates dipped (e.g., lowest noted in 2021) and recovered by 2024; no licensure in 2020 noted. Missing years and institutional heterogeneity complicate longitudinal analysis.
relationship_to_current_study: Highlights need to account for temporal disruptions (e.g., exam cancellations) and cohort-year effects when assembling retrospective datasets for predictive modeling.
limitations_or_cautions: Aggregated data; some table formatting inconsistencies in source extract; institutional heterogeneity indicates caution in generalization.
source_location: Tables 1 and descriptive trends per supplied summary (Pages 2–5)
notes: Plan for missing-year handling and cohort-year covariates.

theme: Overall academic performance and professional-course grades as predictors
subtheme: Study-habits and GWA in RT
study_design: Descriptive-correlational; standardized questionnaire (PSSHI) with institutional GWA
population: 32 BSRT students (AY 2019–2020) from a university in Bulacan, Philippines
variables: Study-habit domains (PSSHI) predictors; GWA professional and general education (outcomes)
key_finding: All eight study-habit domains had positive regression coefficients with GWA but combined effects were not statistically significant (professional GWA R² = 0.246, F = 0.939, p = 0.505). Physical status domain had largest Beta but did not reach p < .05.
relationship_to_current_study: Demonstrates measurement of study-habit constructs in RT samples and that in small single-site samples behavioral predictors may not explain large variance in academic performance; supports focusing on academic records and pre-board scores for licensure prediction.
limitations_or_cautions: Small sample and single site; self-report measures; not linked to licensure outcomes.
source_location: Methods and regression tables per supplied summary (Pages 2–11)
notes: Useful local-discipline background but limited for predicting RTLE.

theme: Pre-board / mock examination performance as predictor of licensure success
subtheme: Feasibility of pairing review-center mock data with PRC outcomes
study_design: Secondary data analysis pairing review center records with PRC results
population: 205 review-center examinees with paired PRC RTLE outcomes (Davao City)
variables: Mock board scores (from review center) paired with PRC licensure outcomes
key_finding: Data were successfully paired and anonymized; ethical procedures reported; demonstrates feasibility of accessing review-center scores and PRC outcomes for predictive analyses.
relationship_to_current_study: Practical precedent for obtaining paired mock and licensure data from local review centers and official PRC records; informs data access and ethical procedures for current study.
limitations_or_cautions: Inclusion/exclusion criteria and handling of repeat examinees are not fully specified in supplied extract.
source_location: Methods: Participants and Measures; Ethical Considerations per supplied summary (Pages 3–4)
notes: Use as procedural precedent for data acquisition and anonymization.

theme: Assessment design, psychometrics, and measurement issues
subtheme: Correction-for-guessing and cognitive-level effects
study_design: Exploratory item-level analysis using AI responses
population: 110 MCQs assembled per ABR blueprint
variables: Raw percent correct, correction-for-guessing adjusted scores, cognitive-level (lower/higher-order)
key_finding: Authors applied correction-for-guessing; reported raw accuracy and corrected-for-guessing adjusted scores; lower-order items had numerically higher correct rates but difference not significant. Demonstrates method for score adjustment and item-level classification.
relationship_to_current_study: Recommend applying correction-for-guessing and cognitive-level classification to institutional pre-board scores when used as predictors to better reflect examinee ability and to perform sensitivity analyses.
limitations_or_cautions: AI context; human test-taking behaviors differ but measurement techniques are applicable.
source_location: Methods and Results (Pages 4–6) and Table 1 per supplied summary
notes: Instruments used (Şenel et al. correction method referenced) — consider reporting adjusted and raw scores.

theme: Program policies, remediation, and review practices
subtheme: Institutional action plans and interventions proposed from readiness studies
study_design: Cross-sectional survey with programmatic recommendations
population: 54 RT graduates (batch 2024)
variables: Self-reported attitude/preparedness/challenges; recommended interventions (mock exams, stress seminars, materials consolidation)
key_finding: Authors propose institutional interventions (e.g., biannual mock exams, stress-coping seminars, peer review lecturers) targeted at improving perceived preparedness; interventions not empirically tested in the paper.
relationship_to_current_study: Interventions provide plausible program responses to predictive findings (e.g., if pre-board identifies at-risk students) and can inform recommendations section.
limitations_or_cautions: Prescriptive, not evaluated.
source_location: Table 7 and Recommendations sections per supplied summary (Pages 7–8)
notes: List of concrete institutional interventions to consider for remediation.

theme: Pre-board / mock examination performance as predictor of licensure success
subtheme: Examples of program-level aggregated prediction (counts vs individual outcomes)
study_design: Institution-level count regression (Poisson/negative binomial)
population: Five RT schools aggregated 2014–2024
variables: Institutional counts and rates (predictors) predicting counts of failed examinees
key_finding: Aggregate predictors (total examinees, institutional passing rate) explained institutional failure counts in some models but were inconsistent across institutions; suggests cohort-level factors matter for institutional planning but not for individual-level prediction.
relationship_to_current_study: Useful for considering higher-level covariates and for planning whether to include institution/cohort-level effects in modeling individual outcomes.
limitations_or_cautions: Aggregate-level analysis cannot replace individual-level predictor modeling; heterogeneity across institutions requires caution.
source_location: Tables 2–6, model descriptions per supplied summary
notes: Consider multi-level models if pooling across cohorts/institutions.
